macro_segments.title: Introduction to Ayin Tachat Ayin and the Nature of Monetary Compensation

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the Rambam's classification of Torah SheBa'al Peh and the problem of Ayin Tachat Ayin
Five categories of Torah SheBa'al Peh: Devarim HaMekubalim, Halacha LeMoshe MiSinai, and sources of Machloket
Context of Perek HaChovel and the five types of compensation

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Dostai ben Yehuda's Proof: The Problem of Unequal Eyes

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Dostai's challenge: if one person has a large eye and another has a small eye, how can Ayin Tachat Ayin mean literal reciprocal injury?
The principle of Mishpat Echad Yihye Lachem requires that the law be fair and equal
Rejection: the concern is vision (nehora), not the physical eye, so the case of different-sized eyes is inapplicable
Comparison to capital cases: one life for another life is considered equivalent, so one vision for another vision should be equivalent too

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Yochai's Proof: The Problem of Impossible Reciprocity

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's case: a blind person blinding someone else, or an amputee amputating someone else's limb
The principle of Mishpat Echad Yihye Lachem requires that the law apply equally; reciprocal punishment is impossible in these cases
Counter-suggestion: apply reciprocal punishment only when possible; when impossible, collect monetary compensation
Rejection via the example of a Treifa (mortally wounded person) killing a healthy person: capital punishment raises complications regarding witnesses

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Proofs from Redundancy in the Text (Tanaim of Rabbi Yishmael and Rabbi Chiya)

micro_segments.title: Tanna of Rabbi Yishmael: the phrase Ki Yinaten Bo is extra and teaches that monetary compensation is required
Rejection of the objection: other similar phrases also appear, so they must be redundant to teach the monetary principle
Tanna of Rabbi Chiya: the phrase Yad BeYad (hand for a hand) means money that is given from hand to hand, not literal hand exchange
Objection: why does the text also say Regel BeRegel (foot for foot) if Yad BeYad already teaches the monetary principle?
Resolution: the phrases are redundant only to teach that monetary compensation is the required form of payment

macro_segments.title: Amoraic Proofs: Abaye, Rav Zvid, Rav Pappa, and the Problem of Equivalence

micro_segments.title: Abaye's proof from the case of a blind person or weak person: if you blinded them, you might kill them due to pain or complications
Counter-suggestion: assess the person beforehand to see if they can tolerate the procedure; only proceed if they can
Rejection by analogy to lashing: if assessment during lashing leads to death, the court is exempt; but that doesn't prove literal punishment is intended
Rav Zvid's proof from Petza (burn): Petza teaches Tzaar (pain and suffering) is paid equivalent to Nezek (damage); therefore monetary compensation is standard
Objection: people have different sensitivities to pain; monetary compensation is needed to equalize unequal suffering
Resolution: since Tzaar must be financial due to individual differences, Nezek (damage) is also financial
Rav Pappa's proof from VeRapo Yerapo (and he shall be healed): medical expenses are paid as compensation, implying monetary compensation
Objection: if literal punishment were intended, both parties would need medical care; why mention medical expenses separately?
Counter-rejection: even if medical expenses are paid, this does not prove that literal punishment is not the primary mode

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Gezeira Shava from Shor: Damages from Damages

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi learns from the word Tachat: just as damages by an ox (Shaleim Yeshaleim Shor Tachat Hashor) are paid in money, so too damages between human beings are paid in money
Objection: why learn from Shor damages rather than from capital crimes (Nefesh Tachat Nefesh)?
Resolution: we learn damages from damages, not from capital cases; human beings from human beings, not from animals
Rav Ashi's alternative: the case of Mitachat Asher Inah (rape damages) shows that Tachat is used for monetary compensation in human-to-human cases

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Dispute: Literal Punishment and the Assessment Problem

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer says Ayin Tachat Ayin means literal injury (Mamash), contradicting the Tannaitic consensus
Rabbah's interpretation: Rabbi Eliezer does not assess as a slave but assesses the actual eye itself
Abaye's objection: a free person has no monetary value, only a slave has a market value
Rav Ashi's resolution: we assess the injurer's eye value (Dmei Mazik), not the injured party's damage (Dmei Nizak), making it more like Kofer

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Assessment Without Slave Markets: The Donkey and Ox Cases

micro_segments.title: Case: a donkey bites off a child's hand; Rav Papa Bar Shmuel orders assessment as a slave
Rava's objection: we don't have Semicha in Bavel to judge Nizakim cases; the child's father refuses assessment on grounds of shame and degradation
Resolution: the debt is deferred until the child grows up and can be assessed
Case: an ox bites off a child's hand; Rava orders assessment as a slave, but the child's father objects on the same grounds

macro_segments.title: The Authority Problem: Nizakim, Semicha, and Shlichusaihu Ka'Avdinan in Bavel

micro_segments.title: Rava's principle: in Bavel, we can only judge Shor BeShur (animal-to-animal) and Shor BeAdam (animal-to-human), but not Adam BeAdam (human-to-human damages)
The reason: Nizakim cases require judges to be called Elohim (judges in place of God), which requires Semicha from the Sanhedrin; Bavel had no such Semicha
For animal cases, the court serves as Shlichusaihu (representatives) of the original Sanhedrin because animals don't require Semicha-level authority
The principle Shlichusaihu Ka'Avdinan: courts can represent the Sanhedrin only in cases that are common (Shachiach) and have monetary loss (Chesron Kis)
Adam BeAdam (human injury) is not considered Shachiach (common) enough to be judged under Shlichusaihu in Bavel
Boshes (embarrassment) is common but has no Chesron Kis (monetary loss in the direct sense), so Shlichusaihu cannot apply
Discussion of Shor cases: Shor Tam (innocent ox) vs. Shor Muad (forewarned ox); complications regarding whether Shor Muad can be established in Bavel
Rav Ashi's conclusion: Shen VeRegel (tooth and foot damages) are Muadim MeTechillatan (forewarned from the start), yet Rava still says they cannot be judged in Bavel

topical_tags.term: Devarim HaMekubalim MiPi Moshe Rabbeinu (transmitted tradition)
